data.title: AT&T: 3 Alternatives To The 6% Yielding Preferreds Before They Move To Junk
data.description:
On our previous coverage of AT&T (T), we suggested that investors dump the common shares for Verizon Communications (VZ). While neither stock has wowed investors with the bubble-like froth of memory chip producers, the switch did help you get 7% better returns over three and a half months.

We go over some of the news flow since then and focus on AT&T's latest acquisitions and how we see that impacting the preferred shares. We also tell you why these preferreds are now so overvalued relative to the alternatives available for higher yields.

Deleveraging Days Are Done

AT&T's big move from the 2023 lows was characterized by getting their balance sheet in the right shape. Trust in management was low (and we are being generous here) and cleaning up the mess was the priority. AT&T was still a bit slow considering how big their debt load was, but it did send the right signals as the company reduced debt by $10.3 billion in 2024, after reducing it by a net $11 billion in 2023.

Alongside that, the company delivered modest EBITDA growth. This got the debt to EBITDA ratios right on track. For AT&T the magic number has always been 2.5X and management commented on this in Q4-2024 conference call.

We also expect to achieve our target of net debt to adjusted EBITDA in the 2.5 times range in the first half of this year. This keeps us on pace to commence common stock repurchases using our initial Board approved tranche of approximately $10 billion during the second half of 2025.

Source: Q4-2024 Conference Call Transcript

Investors were finally on track for a free cash flow yield in the double digits which could work evenly split between dividends and buybacks. Then these things happened.

Acquisition of fiber assets from Lumen:

Announced on May 21, 2025, an agreement to acquire substantially all of Lumen’s Mass Markets fiber internet connectivity business for $5.75 billion in cash, subject to purchase price adjustments.

The transaction is now expected to close in early 2026, subject to certain regulatory and other customary closing conditions.

AT&T will hold the acquired fiber network assets, including certain fiber network deployment capabilities, in a new, fully owned subsidiary (“NetworkCo”).

After closing, the Company plans to sell partial ownership of NetworkCo to an equity partner that will co-invest in the ongoing business. AT&T expects to identify an equity partner and close a transaction within approximately 6-12 months of closing the Lumen transaction.

In the 12-24 months following close, the impact of the transaction is expected to be immaterial to adjusted EBITDA*, adjusted EPS* and free cash flow*, and accretive over the long-term.

Acquisition of wireless spectrum licenses from EchoStar:

Announced on August 26, 2025, the acquisition of approximately 30 MHz of nationwide 3.45 GHz mid-band spectrum and approximately 20 MHz of nationwide 600 MHz low-band spectrum for approximately $23 billion in cash, subject to certain adjustments.

The transaction is expected to close in the first half of 2026, subject to certain closing conditions, including regulatory approvals.

AT&T intends to finance the transaction with cash on hand and incremental borrowings.

Following close, AT&T expects its net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio* to increase to the 3.0x range and return to a level consistent with its leverage target in the 2.5x range within approximately three years.

The Company does not expect a material impact to adjusted EPS* and free cash flow* during the first 24 months following close, with accretion to both metrics expected in the third year.

Source: AT&T Q3-2025 Press Release (emphasis ours)

For those that might not have followed this story in the past, AT&T is notorious for making really, really bad investment decisions. DirectTV is one recent example where the sale price was a fraction of their cost and cumulative capex. While the exact numbers are difficult to extract, we estimate that AT&T lost about $25 billion on this when considering capex and distributions back to it. It is not surprising that investors have gotten lukewarm on the stock since these deals were announced. They have also garnered the attention of the rating agencies.

Fitch rates AT&T Inc. and each rated subsidiaries' Long-Term Issuer Default Ratings 'BBB+'. The RWN applies to all rated IDRs, detailed later in the release, and issue-level ratings except the Short-Term IDR and commercial paper (ratings that are affirmed at 'F2' and would likely remain unchanged if Fitch downgraded the IDR to 'BBB'.

The RWN reflects Fitch's expectations of elevated EBITDA leverage, exceeding Fitch's previous negative sensitivity of 3.2x likely at least through 2027. The transaction closing is anticipated in mid-2026, and Fitch expects to resolve the Rating Watch upon closing of the transaction.

AT&T remains strongly positioned as a U.S. telecom market leader. However, higher leverage combined with a more aggressive capital return strategy heightens the credit risk relative to the company's current rating.

Leverage Moving Higher: EBITDA leverage is slightly elevated near 3.5x at June 2025 and the planned EchoStar spectrum acquisition will further increase leverage to the mid- to high-3.0x range at least through 2027. Fitch expects the spectrum acquisition to be largely debt-financed and follows the company's May 2025 announcement it will acquire fiber assets from Lumen Technologies, Inc. (CCC+/Rating Watch Positive [RWP]) for $5.75 billion. These deals should strengthen AT&T's moat in connectivity offerings but also increases balance sheet risk over the next few years.

Source: Fitch

The Preferred Shares

AT&T has two different preferred shares listed. These are:

1) AT&T Inc. 5% DEP RP PFD A (NYSE:T.PR.A)

2) AT&T Inc. 4.7% DEP SHS PFD C (NYSE:T.PR.C).

They both yield very close to 6%.

Interestingly, these are the ones that would get pushed down in to the "junk" sphere.

While we would not argue that the first rung of junk is catastrophic, we would argue that you are definitely not getting paid for holding these at a 6.06% to 6.13% yield. We will explain why below with examples.

1) We recently wrote about Globe Life Inc. JR SUB DEB (GL.PR.D).

That is not a preferred share, but a debt security with a 6.2% current yield and 6.7% yield to maturity. We will point out that both those numbers exceed the yield from AT&T preferred shares and GL.PR.D is rated BBB+. If AT&T preferred shares are downgraded, GL.PR.D will be 3 notches higher on the rating scale and still will be yielding more.

2) Of course some might argue that they really enjoy the tax advantages of preferred shares versus debt. So the next example is for those investors. RenaissanceRe Holdings Ltd. (RNR) is an A+ rated reinsurance company. Its preferreds, RenaissanceRe Holdings Ltd. 4.20% DEP PFD G (RNR.PR.G) yield 6.5%.

These are also rated the same as GL.PR.D, at BBB+.

3) We had also written about EPR Properties CONV PFD 9% SR E (EPR.PR.E). This one is actually rated lower than AT&T, but you do get a much stronger yield. The stripped yield was around 7.62% when we got in, but that has now come down to a still respectable, 7.05%.

Verdict

AT&T common shares look for middling returns. We owned these once upon a time when they were a bit undervalued, but now we have no desire to return. The preferred shares looked expensive, even when there was no threat of a credit downgrade and now look really expensive relative to the alternatives. We would use this window to move out into better alternatives.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company Deserves To Grow (Upgrade)
data.description:
One of the more perplexing companies right now on the market happens to be The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company (SMG). The last time I wrote about the business was way back in November of 2020. Even though I found it to be a fascinating prospect, I took issue with how expensive units were. There was some optimism around the company, especially in light of the strong revenue growth that it had experienced over the couple of years leading up to that point. But that was not enough to justify a bullish assessment to me.

Since rating it a Hold back then, the market ended up pushing the stock down 59.5%. By comparison, the S&P 500 (SP500) is up an astounding 93.2%. Given this massive return disparity, you might think that the fundamental picture for the business had collapsed. But that's not exactly what transpired. Yes, revenue has fallen some. But the good news is that profitability has improved significantly. Cash flows are up, and the stock is looking cheaper now than it did back then. When you add all of this together, I believe that upgrading it to a soft Buy is absolutely appropriate.

Part of being a sophisticated investor is being open to new information as it comes in. The good news for investors is that this new information is about to come out. Before the market opens on January 28th, the management team at The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. As we approach that date, analysts unfortunately are pessimistic when it comes to revenue. And on profitability, they view a mixed bag. I do think investors would be wise to carefully evaluate the data when management does release it. Because at the end of the day, that could justify a rather quick downgrade if things don't go well.

Taking A Fresh Look At The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company

Whenever I go an extended period of time without analyzing a company, I like to dig into its operations before getting into the fundamentals. This helps me to re familiarize myself with the business, and I believe it is useful for my readers as well. Conceptually, The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company is a rather simple business. The firm focuses on branded consumer lawn and garden products in North America, and it also provides certain nutrients, lighting, and other materials, that are used for indoor and hydroponic gardening activities. This is a rather old business, dating all the way back to 1868 in my home state of Ohio. Its origins back then involved the production and distribution of lawn fertilizers and grass seeds. But today, we have a rather sizeable business, with a market capitalization of $3.42 billion.

Perhaps the best way to understand the company is to look at its operating segments. First and foremost, we have it U.S. Consumer segment, which focuses on things such as lawn care products, gardening products, and landscape products. Examples here include lawn fertilizers, water-soluble plant foods, and the like. The Hawthorne segment, meanwhile, is focused more on lighting aimed at helping growers engage in hydroponic and indoor gardening, as well as controls like insect control products.

Over the last few years now, overall financial performance for the business has been mixed. Revenue, for instance, has been a bit disappointing, dropping from $3.55 billion in 2023 to $3.41 billion in 2025. According to management, this drop in revenue was driven by a plunge in sales associated with its Hawthorne segment. Revenue plummeted from $467.3 million to $165.8 million. This is, I believe, the primary source of the company's problems. According to management, this drop was driven by lower sales volume and lower pricing. The drop in sales volume has been the primary culprit. And a lot of this has to do with the cannabis industry. That market has become oversupplied. In essence, because of a flurry of investment activity years ago, there was a huge buildup of capacity for growing there. Eventually, this caught up with the space, pushing the demand for hydroponic systems, lights, nutrients, and other related items, all lower.

It is important to keep in mind that the drop in volume would necessarily push pricing lower also. Lower demand justifies that. It's also important to note that management has been making some strategic decisions here. They have specifically scaled back the distribution of third-party brands while focusing more on their own product lines. And on top of this, they have also made the decision to exit certain product lines entirely. Management has also been pushing to sell off this part of the company in the near future. In all likelihood, that will occur early this year.

As disappointing as this might be for those interested in the space, it is worth noting that the decline in revenue here brought with it improvements in profitability. In 2023, Hawthorne reported losses of $48.1 million. But in 2025, it achieved a $2.8 million profit. So this right sizing of the business is working in favor of investors, even if it means that The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company will no longer be a cannabis centric play. This does help to explain why the share price has fallen. But when we look at everything else, the picture looks great.

Even as revenue for the company declined, the larger operating segment, the U.S. Consumer segment, reported a slight increase in revenue from $2.84 billion to $2.99 billion. This increase, according to the company, seems to have been driven by higher sales volumes for the most part. However, from 2024 to 2025, there was a bit of weakness caused by lower pricing, with management attributing at least some of that to investments aimed at consumer activation activities. I know that sounds fancy, but it's really just discounting in order to draw in new customers. As this occurred, profits for the segment did improve, rising from $454.7 million in 2023 to $572.6 million in 2025.

The slow winding down of Hawthorne, combined with the improvements that we have seen when it comes to the U.S. Consumer segment, have had a big impact on the bottom line for the company. It went from a net loss of $380.1 million in 2023 to a net profit of $145.2 million in 2025. Adjusted net profits, meanwhile, shot up from $68.1 million to $219.6 million. Other profitability metrics large I improved, with the one exception being operating cash flow. It worsened from $531 million to $371.3 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $112.3 million to $402.2 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business popped from $446.9 million to $581.1 million.

One thing that has also helped has been a reduction in overall net debt. Back in 2023, the company had net debt of $2.58 billion. But since then, the firm has reduced overall net debt to $2.07 billion. This has helped push down the interest expense for the company from 5.02% of sales to 3.77%. At first glance, this might seem minuscule. But when applied to the revenue generated in 2025, that's an extra $42.7 million in pre-tax savings. That is absolutely something to write home about.

Ideally, this should better position the company to capitalize on market opportunities. Right now, it's estimated that the consumer do-it-yourself lawn and garden space is worth about $11 billion. Around half of that is centered around control products like weed killer, insect control products, and rodent control products. But the rest of it involves garden related offerings like soils, mulch, and plant food, as well as fertilizers, grass seeds, and spreaders that all fall under the lawn category.

There are some areas that the company is focused on when it comes to 2026 specifically. For instance, it is continuing to push for the launch of solution type products under the feeding category. It's targeting the new do-it-yourself lawn care consumers with products dedicated to the younger generations. And it is investing in certain innovation and renewed advertising activities in order to grow its grass seed business. This is a huge area of opportunity when it comes to the lawn category. But that's not all. Management is also investing in the gardens space through the launch of products dedicated things like indoor and organics, and it is also investing more heavily into the controls' category, specifically with the launch of 10 new products under its Ortho brand, and additional emphasis on its e-commerce channels.

When it comes to 2026, management expects adjusted earnings per share to come in at between $4.15 and $4.35. At the midpoint, we would be looking at adjusted net profits of $245.2 million, which would be comfortably above the $219.6 million the company reported for 2025. And with EBITDA margin expected to rise at a mid-single digit rate, we are likely to see EBITDA for 2026 of around $610.2 million, which would imply adjusted operating cash flow of about $449.1 million.

Using these estimates, as well as the historical results from the last two fiscal years, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are rather low multiples if you ask me. However, relative to other similar firms, the stock is more in the fair value range.

In the chart below, I stacked it up against five similar enterprises:

  • Central Garden & Pet Company (CENT).
  • The Mosaic Company (MOS).
  • Nutrien Ltd. (NTR).
  • Corteva, Inc. (CTVA).
  • FMC Corporation (FMC).

Two of them were cheaper on a price to earnings basis. Three of them were cheaper when it came to the price to operating cash flow approach. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, three were cheaper while another was tied with it.

Earlier in this article, I mentioned that management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Those results are forecasted to come out before the market opens on January 28th. Unfortunately, there is the expectation that revenue will fall, with analysts forecasting a decline from $416.8 million last year to $356.6 million this year. But when it comes to profitability, the picture is going to be mixed. They expect, for instance, for a loss per share of $0.97. That would actually be an improvement over the $1.21 per share that the company lost a year earlier. And it would mean an improvement in profitability from a loss of $69.5 million to $56.9 million.

Adjusted earnings per share are a different animal entirely. Analysts expect a loss per share of $1.01. That compares to the $0.89 in per share earnings lost the previous year. And it would mean adjusted net losses growing from $51 million to $59.3 million. I would venture to say that adjusted earnings will be more representative of the overall health of the company. And with revenue falling, it's not unthinkable that the cash flow metrics will come in worse year over year as well. In the chart above, you can see what these figures were last year. And I would urge investors to go over these data points carefully when the news is released.

Takeaway

While The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company might not be the cheapest candidate when it comes to companies that touch on these end markets, the firm is most certainly an attractive prospect on an absolute basis. It's foray into the cannabis market unfortunately did not pan out. But I see that as only a mild inconvenience considering what has happened from a profit and cash flow perspective over the last few years now. And given the innovations that management is focused on, I believe that additional growth is likely. Because of all of this, I believe that upgrading it to a soft ‘buy’ is the right choice.

data.title: Stride, Inc.: Tech Issues Stabilizing Will Drive Stock Price Appreciation
data.description:
Thesis: Operations May Stabilize This Year Which Will Drive Stock Price Appreciation

I believe that the sell-off of Stride, Inc. (LRN) stock, over 50% since its pre-Q1 peak, may be overdone, based on my primary research and industry expertise as a practitioner in the K-12 virtual school market.

The valuation of less than 5x EBITDA most likely reflects skepticism surrounding the stability of the business given a challenged learning management system (LMS) Canvas implementation and to a lesser extent a Student Information System (SIS) PowerSchool implementation at partner schools.

While highly damaging to the brand and overall user experience, my research suggests that the user experience has stabilized. Stride, owner of K12 which operates virtual school solutions, is the leader in the market that it serves. It will continue to serve as the leader well into the future despite this operational hiccup in my opinion.

I come to this assessment with an unusual level of familiarity with the virtual-school sector relative to other Seeking Alpha authors. I previously served as the Head of Strategy at Connections Education, now Pearson Online & Blended Learning, for more than six years (2012-2018). Pearson is Stride’s top competitor in virtual school instruction. I followed this company back in 2007 as a sell-side analyst after it IPOd and have had a twenty year history with it as an investor and competitor.

While I respect those who contribute opinions on Stride on this platform, my sense is that others reflexively said to buy the stock because it declined by 50% and the business was intact. Perhaps that’s all that is needed to know when investing. I believe many do not fully appreciate the structural complexity of this business. Perhaps hearing from an industry insider might help those who are still kicking the tires on the story.

Stride serves two types of customers: the families/learners who enroll and the school partners that have the contractual relationship with Stride. To value the stock, you must understand the stickiness or lack thereof of both.

The bear thesis on this stock presumably is that forward estimates are uncertain given the possibility of churn by both enrollments and partner schools given the challenged tech implementation this summer. It might be hyperbolic to characterize the rollout as a disaster, but then again the stock did see a 50% price decline. Management chose to migrate all schools simultaneously without running a pilot lasting for an entire year. I understand why management may have chosen to do this, better to rip the band-aid off all at once rather than run duplicative systems with duplicative processes that are highly inefficient.

At Pearson circa 2018 we had our own challenged upgrade to our education management system but we applied it to only 10% of our revenue. Even then the rollout was highly challenged. I empathize with Stride management and its employees. Based on time as a practitioner, I can categorically say that both companies are well-managed with committed employees who want to do the right thing by their students, families, and partners. In investing in edtech stocks culture matters particularly given that these are services businesses. I wouldn't have purchased Stride stock if I wasn't confident in their corporate values in trying to do the right thing by learners.

Based on my review of parent sentiment from social media posts and reaching out on social media to some, I believe that the situation shifted from crisis to nuisance.

The company trades at a steep discount relative to the historical trading range. The implementation failures damaged goodwill and created meaningful operational disruption in August, September, and October. Enrollment guidance came in at the lower end of expectations of 10-15% enrollment growth that the company previously guided to. The company posted 11.3% enrollment growth in Q1.

My thesis is that operations have stabilized. If the company posts several good quarters the stock potentially sees its valuation multiple expand.

The company will report Q2 results on January 27th. I have little sense if Q2 estimates are conservative or aggressive. Investors in my opinion will reward stabilization. I put money into the stock ahead of the print because of my perception of an asymmetric return. Downside in my opinion is largely priced in (particularly given the less than 5x EV/EBITDA valuation multiple).

Stride's K12 Business is the Leader in a Growing Market

The addressable market for virtual schooling expanded dramatically after the pandemic, with millions of families becoming aware that alternatives to traditional brick-and-mortar schools exist. Previously at Pearson pre-pandemic we thought that the addressable market was 3% of a total student population in a given state. The pandemic created a new reality where people became familiar with virtual schooling. Not necessarily best practices of how to operate a virtual school program, but at minimum the concept.

The new market reality post-pandemic has unclear upper bounds. Currently, market penetration of virtual schooling stands at less than 2% of the student population of the United States. The natural question is, how large can the penetration rate of full-time virtual instruction grow to? No one, including industry insiders, knows.

Enrollment growth can potentially mature next year.

Alternatively, the upside could be quite large.

Students typically attend a virtual school not because it's a better form of instruction but because families are trying to solve problems. 1 out of five students in the United States are neurodiverse. The traditional brick-and-mortar experience is horrific for these students. I know this firsthand given my own child’s challenges at her 2E program, where noise sensitivity materially influences the day-to-day brick-and-mortar environment. Even if the peak addressable market is double or triple the current market, this stock is a buy given the possibility of double-digit revenue growth over the next several years.

In this market, the largest scaled operators are Stride's K12 and Pearson's Connections Academy.

Schools that contract with the likes of Stride and Pearson do not shift to other turnkey vendors. They make the decision to in-source key capabilities. It is quite difficult to shift operations to other vendors. There are many reasons for this, but a key one is that many of the charter school operators are nonprofits that operate at a deficit. Operators like Stride and Pearson reduce their listed price up until the school achieves an account balance. Schools that leave their turnkey providers tend to have positive account balances. They have financial flexibility to in-source operational, regulatory, teaching, and technology capabilities.

One last comment: there is a misperception still that Stride's offering enables homeschooling. That is a different market altogether. Stride provides services to school districts. Students at schools that Stride supports are part of the school system albeit learning from home. Homeschooling is a consumer pay market where students are unaffiliated with the school district. The pandemic caused an increase of homeschoolers from the traditional 3% of the US population to closer to 10%. The number of homeschool students has not exactly reverted to its average, with the % of students in the 2022-2023 school year standing at close to 6%. This is another example of the pandemic changing family behaviors and engaging in a willingness to explore options that are in the best interests of the child.

Enrollment and Partner School Retention — Two Key Issues for Investors

There are only two questions that matter for Stride’s long-term valuation:

  • will students churn at elevated rates?
  • will partner schools terminate their contracts?

Student Retention: Frustration in August/September But Potential Stabilization Since Then

Management said that 10-15K fewer enrollments materialized this summer than they otherwise anticipated because of the technology systems. I believe that students who churned did so in that narrow window. After September, I saw no evidence of large-scale withdrawals per social media posts. Parents distinguished clearly between annoying and unacceptable. Despite the operational mess, most families view Stride as dramatically better than their local district option.

Partner-School Retention: No Evidence of Impending Departures

Stride does not own schools. Rather, it operates them on behalf of school districts and charter schools. Bears perhaps assume that these schools may abandon Stride due to the technology issues. Institutional investors around long enough might remember when K12’s Agora Cyber Charter School announced that it would leave Stride as a managed school back in 2014. The Agora board announced that they elected to use an RFP process to find other vendors. Largely because of Agora, Stride's stock declined from roughly $30 in September 2013 to less than $10 by 2016. Agora represented at that time 13% of the company’s total revenue. It took Agora multiple years to extricate itself from being a Stride-managed school with the gradual removal of services.

Customer concentration back in the 2010s was THE problem in Stride’s business model. Stride invested in marketing dollars to attract students and increased enrollments at schools to the point at which a school would be large enough to leave Stride. Management responded to this issue by launching/managing multiple schools in a state. If a school seeks to leave Stride, parents have the option of going to another school and using the same curriculum. K12 solved this problem of customer concentration. Currently no customer represents more than 10% of revenue.

Investors need to be aware that states have materially different funding levels. The schools in the states with large funding for students/virtual students are the most susceptible for partner schools to leave.

Pennsylvania has better K-12 virtual school funding than any other state. It is no accident that K12 does not currently fully operate a turnkey management school in Pennsylvania. Interestingly, Insight PA dropped its management contract with K12 at the end of the last school year. It did not materially affect the financials of Stride because of the massive growth in the overall market. It's another sign that customer concentration may not be the threat that it once was for this company.

From an industry insider perspective, I believe that Stride management has done an exceptional job in having an operating model for the schools that it manages and has done well in fidelity to implementation.

I came to this conclusion after I virtually attended several publicly available board meetings for large Stride-operated schools, including the California Virtual Academies. If partner schools were preparing to leave, there would be signs:

  • agenda items focusing on Canvas/PowerSchool
  • requests for remediation plans
  • discussion of replacing Stride/RFPs
  • board-member commentary on the relationship with Stride and critiques

None of this occurred in the virtual board meetings that I attended. Investors are more than welcome to look at the publicly available board meeting minutes and agendas. Visit the link here, for example, for the California December board agenda.

Had investors reviewed the agendas before the company reported results, they would have been aware of the Canvas LMS issues back in August/September before the company reported. It tells you the importance of understanding the board dynamics when investing in this stock.

The LMS issues over the past several months did not appear as agenda items, at least in California schools managed by Stride. While public commenters, including the California teacher union leader, expressed legitimate concerns, board members did not place the LMS issues on the board agenda.

Partner schools also may suffer from migration fatigue. For partner schools to extricate themselves from Stride, they would have to contract with multiple providers to make up the various services that Stride provides. The partners would have to contract with an LMS provider, which more likely than not would be Canvas. I can't imagine that families would be interested in migrating to yet another version of Canvas, which will have the same sort of bugs that they experienced this past year. My sense is that partners are unlikely to inflict another LMS transition on families so soon.

I heard from the board meetings that the boards were alerted in the springtime of the shift. The board commentary I observed suggests alignment with Stride, not the beginning of a separation.

Estimates and Valuation

Revenue for much of Stride depends on a September enrollment count at its managed schools. This means that there likely won't be a material deviation from the consensus revenue estimate. Revenue is slated to grow by 4.4% YoY.

The CFO-turned-CEO has historically been conservative with guidance. The presumption is that the CEO appropriately introduced a level of conservatism in the financial outlook as has been his practice. To be clear, risk exists that the company misses their estimates. Revenue at least this year will not deviate materially from the consensus estimate.

As you can see from the chart below, revenue accelerated in fiscal 2025 to close to 18% YoY. The current year estimates represent a material deceleration of revenue to 4%. Fiscal year 2027 (not shown in the table below) is at 5% YoY revenue growth according to Yahoo Finance.

My expectation is that several years post-pandemic enrollment can grow 5-10% YoY, reflecting modest 1-2% increases in state funding and modest enrollment growth at existing schools. Stride made some problematic acquisitions (e.g., bootcamp provider Galvanize) that in aggregate saw a 30% YoY decline in revenue just in Q1. These acquisitions likely lower revenue growth relative to enrollment growth. Research entities suggest that the virtual school market will grow double digits over the next several years. One should take this with a grain of salt what research firms say, as no one really knows how fast the market will grow post-pandemic. That being said, Stride is growing at a slower growth rate than what it was otherwise capable of given the tech issues (as evidenced by the enrollment churn in August/September). Its not inconceivable that growth accelerates next year with tech problems resolved.

Stride’s historical EBITDA multiple at times exceeded 10×. That demonstrates the upside from today’s depressed levels of 4.7× EBITDA.

The company needs to regain the trust of investors to regain its prior valuation multiple. Edtech stocks typically trade between 5-15x EBITDA.

So why did this previously warrant a 10x multiple? Stride has the following:

  • Leading operator in the market that it serve
  • Post-pandemic market-driven awareness
  • Proprietary curriculum, which creates consumer lock-in (assuming operational challenges)

The larger story is intact. None of this has changed.

I value this stock at $140/share, close to 10x EBITDA, what the stock used to trade at before the technology issues. Effectively this is what the stock traded at prior to the technology issues affecting the current year results.

Risks

Investors new to this story need to understand that the risks associated with this stock are meaningful. I suspect high volatility with the stock price in either direction when the company reports their Q2 results on January 27th.

Partner schools could leave over the next two years given the failed implementation. It could take multiple years for schools to exit their relationship with Stride (with services reduced each year). This risk is somewhat mitigated by the fact that it would cause the school to have to go through another painful migration

Partner schools could reduce the level of services they buy from K12.

Scrutiny of virtual schools by media outlets, think tanks, and politicians is also possible. Think tanks used to publish on poor academic outcomes of virtual schools. Stride needs to invest to improve student outcomes. Unlike for-profit higher ed that investors may be familiar with, K-12 institutions like school districts and charter schools have to demonstrate real and tangible academic outcomes.

There was a lawsuit filed in New Mexico late summer, and potential contagion of allegations could prove an issue. Investors in for-profit education need to be aware of headline risk driving stock price performance.

LMS instability could also persist longer than expected.

Also, AI may compress pricing. Stride signs long-term contracts with its partners. Over time, though, AI might reduce the perceived value of the curriculum. Conceivably, at some point new curriculum providers can emerge that can use generative AI to recreate virtual school curriculum that aligns with state standards. AI cannot replace the services that Stride provides, but the curriculum is one area of concern that I have. This is not an immediate risk, though.

Conclusion: Risk Exists, but Asymmetric Risk/Reward Presents an Opportunity

Stride’s business model is not broken. Enrollment churn in the summer happened during a crisis. Operational issues with the LMS calmed down, according to my primary research.

If Q2, Q3, and Q4 earnings reports confirm enrollment stabilization, as my research suggests is plausible, the valuation multiple of the stock could expand.

Stocks that decline by 50% do so because the market is pricing in risk. Yes, real risk exists for this name, and investors should consider that when contemplating buying shares. My sense is that it's not as bad as it appears. We will find out soon enough when the company reports its results on January 27th at 5pm EST.

Today’s valuation represents a compelling entry point for long-term investors.

Summary

  • The Polen Credit U.S. Opportunistic High Yield Composite (DDJIX) underperformed both the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index and the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index (net of fees) in the fourth quarter.
  • During the quarter, high yield bond yields fell as investors absorbed two more Fed rate cuts. Although leveraged loans lagged their fixed rate peers, a resilient economy and a dovish Fed were viewed positively by loan investors.
  • Material Sciences and Asurion contributed the most to total returns, while Oldcastle BuildingEnvelope and Realtruck Group were the largest detractors during the quarter.
  • Over the period, portfolio positioning did not change materially. Most trading activity involved purchases and sales in existing holdings.
  • Despite tariff uncertainty and the prolonged government shutdown, we believe that the U.S. macro environment remains constructive, supported by policy tailwinds, robust AI-related investment, and a durable though increasingly uneven consumer and corporate backdrop.
  • Given historically tight spreads but still-attractive yields, active managers need to look beyond headline valuations to identify idiosyncratic opportunities, using discipline during market dislocations to capitalize on inefficiencies over time.

The gross performance returns for the Polen Credit U.S. Opportunistic High Yield Composite set forth above are gross of all fees except for trading expenses, deal-related legal expenses, and withholding taxes. Net returns reflect the application of actual management and performance-based fees to gross returns. Returns of the Polen Credit U.S. Opportunistic High Yield Composite are provided as supplemental information and complement a GIPS Composite report, which has been provided. The commentary is not intended to guarantee profitable outcomes. Any forward-looking statements are based on certain expectations and assumptions susceptible to changes in circumstances. Opinions and views expressed constitute the judgment of Polen Capital as of the date herein and may involve assumptions and estimates which are not guaranteed and are subject to change.

Commentary

During the quarter, high yield bond yields fell as investors absorbed two more Fed rate cuts. High yield spreads remain sub-300 basis points and tightened into quarter end, after initially widening in early October driven by trade war induced uncertainty. A reversal of these concerns along with the end of the government shutdown and resilient macroeconomic data signaling better-than-expected growth all supported risk assets into year-end. The ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index produced a gain of 1.35% during the quarter. High yield market gains were led by BB-rated bonds, which produced a gain of 1.57%, while B-rated bonds gained 1.55% and CCC-rated bonds produced a loss of 0.52%. In addition, during the quarter, the top performing sectors were Healthcare, Utility and Automotive, while the biggest laggards were the Media, Technology & Electronics and Basic Industry sectors.

Meanwhile, although leveraged loans lagged their fixed rate peers, a resilient economy and a dovish Fed were viewed positively by investors. Additional support for the asset class came from a strong technical bid from elevated CLO origination. During the quarter, the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index produced a gain of 1.19%, as BB-rated loans in the index outperformed their B-rated peers, with each producing gains of 1.68% and 1.20%, while CCC-rated loans generated a loss of 0.94%. Lastly, in Q4 2025, the Aerospace, Retail and Metals & Minerals sectors in the loan market were the top performers. Conversely, the Chemicals, Food & Drug and Transportation sectors were the biggest laggards.

During the quarter, primary market activity tapered into year-end and Q4 proved to be the lightest quarter for new issue activity in 2025. High yield bond issuance totaled just $65.4 billion and was dominated by refinancing activity. New issue activity of leveraged loans recorded $171.3 billion, a drop-off of about 50% from the previous quarter’s total. In addition, like the high yield bond market, refinancing and repricing was the dominant use of proceeds accounting for a combined 70% of all loan new issue activity. Further, as mentioned previously, CLO activity remained elevated.

Finally, according to data from J.P. Morgan, default activity in Q4 2025 was the largest total in 2025 and included five of the largest defaults/distressed exchanges of the year. With that noted, the trailing twelve-month default rates for high yield bonds and leveraged loans (including distressed exchanges and liability management exercises “LMEs”) sat at 1.88% and 2.87%, respectively. However, excluding distressed exchanges and LMEs, default rates fell to 0.99% and 1.46%.

Portfolio Performance & Attribution

The Polen Credit U.S. Opportunistic High Yield Composite generated a total return of -0.07% gross and -0.18% net of fees during the fourth quarter. The Composite underperformed the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index by 142 bps gross and 153 bps net of fees.

In Q4 2025, U.S. Treasury yields declined. Because the Portfolio maintains a shorter duration than the benchmark, this move detracted from relative performance. However, the Portfolio’s higher income level versus the benchmark helped offset some of this impact and contributed positively to relative results.

Looking at attribution by rating, the quality allocation effect was negative, driven primarily by the Portfolio’s overweight to bonds and loans rated CCC2 and CCC3. In addition, the Portfolio’s aggregate security selection effect by rating was negative. Specifically, the Portfolio’s CCC2-rated, CCC3-rated and B1-rated holdings lagged those of the benchmark and detracted from relative performance. These negative effects were partially offset by the Portfolio’s CCC1-rated and B3-rated holdings, which outperformed those of the benchmark and contributed to relative performance.

Further, sector attribution shows that the sector security selection effect was negative. This negative effect was driven by the Portfolio’s holdings in the Basic Industry, Automotive and Healthcare sectors, which lagged those of the benchmark. These negative effects were partially offset by the positive effects produced by the Portfolio’s holdings in the Retail and Services sectors, which outperformed those of the benchmark and contributed to relative performance.

Notable issuers that contributed to, or detracted from, the Portfolio’s total return for the quarter are set forth below.

Top Contributors

Material Sciences Corporation provides proprietary multi-material metal processing solutions for niche coated, laminated, and acoustical applications. The Portfolio holds the company’s SOFR + 8.5% Second Lien Notes due 2030. The company continues to perform well operationally, remains on track with its 2026 deleveraging plan, and reported strong free cash flow in Q3. We believe the position continues to offer an attractive risk-reward profile.

Source: ICE, Credit Suisse, J.P. Morgan. Portfolio information provided is based on a representative account of the Polen Credit U.S. Opportunistic High Yield Composite. The representative account is an account within the Polen Credit U.S. Opportunistic High Yield Composite that Polen Capital Credit has deemed the most representative of the accounts managed by Polen Capital pursuing the investment strategy.

Asurion is the leading provider of mobile device protection plans in the U.S. and Japan, supported by its scale, recurring subscription revenue, and low capital intensity. Its dominant position— supplying phone insurance to two of the three major U.S. wireless carriers—helps drive stable cash flow and strong customer retention. In December 2025, Asurion announced the GBP 2.1 billion acquisition of Domestic & General, which broadens geographic reach, supports growth in the Connected Home offering, and provides potential synergies. To fund the transaction, the company issued a $3.3 billion first-lien bond due 2032—the first bond deal in its history—allowing it to extend near-term maturities and diversify funding sources. These developments contributed positively to the performance of the SOFR + 5.25% Second Lien Term Loan due 2029.

Largest Detractors

Oldcastle BuildingEnvelope is a North American manufacturer and distributor of architectural glass, aluminum framing systems, and related hardware for commercial and residential applications. In 2022, KPS acquired the company from CRH for $3.6B (~10.9x LTM EBITDA). Oldcastle is operating in a competitive market and facing weaker earnings due to lower volumes, margin pressure, and operational missteps following the unusually strong 2022– 2023 environment. These headwinds have increased leverage, and we do not expect earnings to recover until 2027. However, we believe Oldcastle has sufficient liquidity to help bridge to an improved earnings environment and positive cash flow in 2027. In addition, we view the CR Laurence segment as a valuable asset that could be sold at a low-double-digit to mid-teens EBITDA multiple to reduce leverage if needed. While leverage through Oldcastle's 9.50% Senior Notes due 2030 is higher than originally anticipated, we believe the securities have the potential to offer an attractive return from current prices and anticipate that the capital structure may be refinanced in 2028 in connection with an improved earnings profile or a possible sale of the business.

RealTruck is a leading U.S. manufacturer and distributor of aftermarket accessories for pickup trucks, SUVs, and jeeps, and operates realtruck.com—the largest dedicated online retailer of aftermarket truck parts in the country. In Q4 2025, the Portfolio’s 6.25% Senior Notes due 2029 traded lower amid concerns about softer U.S. consumer spending. We believe RealTruck remains relatively insulated from tariff impacts and may gain market share from competitors that rely heavily on low-cost imports. Despite potential near-term volatility, the company’s margin profile, brand strength, and junior equity cushion support continued confidence in the position, which the Portfolio maintains while closely monitoring developments.

Portfolio Positioning & Activity

We did not make any significant changes to portfolio positioning during the quarter. However, we increased positions in certain existing holdings and initiated several new positions, one of which is described below. Lastly, we executed a few sales, one of which is also described below.

In Q4 2025, we initiated a position in the Portfolio in TeraWulf’s 7.75% First Lien Secured Notes due 2030. TeraWulf (WULF) is a U.S.-based digital asset technology company transitioning from bitcoin mining to developing high-performance computing and AI data-center infrastructure. The company has entered long-term lease agreements with Fluidstack, which are backstopped by AA-rated Google once the data centers are completed. Several projects are already underway, and visibility toward full completion is strong. The Notes benefit from a lock-box structure that directs contracted revenues to required operating expenses and debt service, along with an amortizing feature that accelerates deleveraging. Given the combination of yield, structure, and improving business fundamentals, we believe the notes offer an attractive risk-reward opportunity.

We reduced the Portfolio’s position in Sotera’s 7.375% Senior Notes due 2031 during the quarter, taking advantage of strong price appreciation. Sotera Health (SHC) is a vertically integrated provider of sterilization and lab testing services for the pharmaceutical and medical device industries, operating through three segments: Sterigenics, Nordion, and Nelson Labs. Sterigenics sterilizes finished medical devices, Nordion supplies Cobalt-60 used in gamma sterilization, and Nelson Labs provides advisory and lab testing services. Sotera operates 65 facilities across 13 countries, primarily in North America with additional locations in Europe, Asia, and South America.

Despite recent disruptions, including tariff uncertainty and a prolonged U.S. government shutdown, we believe that the macro- economic backdrop is broadly supportive. Economic growth continues in the U.S., aided by accommodative fiscal and monetary policy. The “One Big Beautiful Bill Act,” AI-driven capital expenditures, and a healthy consumer support this contention. Although still above the Federal Reserve’s target, inflation in the U.S. has stabilized. With labor markets providing mixed signals, the Fed may choose to pause additional policy accommodation, at least for the time being.

Further, the U.S. consumer remains a focal point. Although headline numbers remain encouraging, consumption continues to bifurcate. Lower income cohorts remain strained, while higher income consumers continue to spend. Similarly, corporate performance shows a comparable divergence, and although fundamentals remain healthy overall, the benefits of the AI boom have been uneven across sectors. Tariff concerns have also created pockets of pressure, though many of the worst-case scenarios from early 2025 have been mitigated. High yield and leveraged loan spreads are currently tight relative to history.

Despite tight spreads, yields remain attractive compared to longer-term averages and relative to other fixed-income sectors. Given the tight spread environment, active managers must look beyond headline metrics to uncover opportunities to add incremental yield. Regardless of where average spreads currently reside, idiosyncratic opportunities remain for long term investors to exploit.

We remain committed to delivering compelling, risk-adjusted returns for our clients. We continue to seek out high-quality businesses that offer attractive risk-reward where our research indicates the business is well positioned to navigate market corrections and meet their debt obligations. Although it can be uncomfortable at times, temporary mark-to-market fluctuations often create exceptional buying opportunities as market inefficiencies emerge.

While those disruptive periods have become increasingly shorter in duration, we remain poised to act when they do occur. By staying committed to our process and remaining agile in deploying capital, we believe that our clients’ portfolios are well positioned to capitalize on these dislocations, ultimately adding to performance over the long term.

Sincerely,

Ben Santonelli, and John Sherman

The Polen Credit U.S. Opportunistic High Yield Composite ("the Composite") was created in August 2007. The U.S. Opportunistic High Yield strategy seeks to generate capital appreciation and income by investing in high yield securities or higher rated securities that offer yields similar to those available in the high yield market. The strategy focuses on investments in high yield bonds and has a bias toward lower tier securities. Opportunistic High Yield portfolios not denominated in U.S. dollars, where currency hedging is a significant component of the strategy, are excluded from the Composite. Derivatives may be used for hedging purposes only; however, certain credit derivatives may be used in limited circumstances subject to client guidelines. In January 2025, following an internal review of the portfolios included within the Polen Credit U.S. Opportunistic High Yield Composite, Polen Capital has redefined such Composite to exclude, effective January 1, 2024, portfolios that maintain an exposure to illiquid securities in excess of 35% of assets for a consecutive three-month period.
