data.title: 3 Top Picks For 2026 (And The Next Decade)
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

At the beginning of the year, there are a lot of analysts and writers who come out with their "top picks". I haven't done that myself, and I don't really intend to start. In general, I'm always on the 'hunt' for what I believe are opportunities, and that tends to be during corrections or bear markets.

However, it is a new year where I can contribute additional cash to my Roth IRA once again. At this point, I'm already sitting on what I believe is a substantial level of cash. Being that I'm at a comfortable level already, I wanted to go ahead and make some additional direct purchases rather than continue to build up an even larger cash hoard.

On the other hand, I wouldn't blame someone wanting to sit on an even larger cash pile, given that the market is already off to the races for the new year. We've already been hitting new all-time highs as a continuation of last year's momentum seems to be hanging on—well, we can really call it the last 3 years running now.

With all that said, I'd really think of these purchases not particularly as "top picks for 2026" (but the title might get more clicks); instead, I'd consider these more as "top picks for the next decade." These are also all three names that are already names I hold in my portfolio, so not new positions, but adding to the positions.

This piece will act as a "TRADE ALERT" article, as there will be names I'll be adding later today.

#1 Alphabet (GOOGL)

This is a name that is most definitely not in the 'value' bucket, as it is less than 1% off its 52 and all-time high levels at the time of writing. I originally purchased my first batch of shares of GOOGL, going back to August 5, 2024. At that time, I also wrote some long-dated short puts, too. At that time, shares were trading at around $158 and at about 20x forward P/E.

Today, it is sitting at over 30x forward P/E. Those short puts we ended up closing out early at a huge profit, being realized. Of course, it is hard to complain about a huge profit—however, buying more aggressively at that time would have been an even better bet. It was just the more cautious way to approach it.

For what it is worth, it does receive a fair value price from Morningstar of $340 and an average price target of ~$335 from other Wall Street analysts.

Of course, as share prices fluctuate, analysts often adjust their price targets up and down, depending on the trajectory of the market. With that said, this is really why I wanted to clarify that this is a next-decade or multi-decade type holding. Over the next several years, earnings are expected to grow quite rapidly.

Therefore, something that might look richly valued at ~30x earnings and nearly 9x forward sales but growing rapidly doesn't look so expensive a few years out. Given the long time frame and this name being one of the clear leaders in the AI revolution, I'm comfortable adding a handful of shares to this name—it'll be averaging up, but during the next correction or bear market, there could always be the opportunity to add even more aggressively.

In 2024, Alphabet started paying a dividend to investors as well. They raised the payout by 5% (a penny bump), which was rather underwhelming considering the significant free cash flow the company generates.

I had been hoping they'd be a bit more aggressive like Microsoft (MSFT), with its ~10% 10-year CAGR for its dividend. So at least for now, it appears they are going the Apple (AAPL) route of penny raises, but time will tell.

#2 American States Water Company (AWR)

Now here is a name that is looking like a relative value play. It is a water utility company that is trading nearly 12% off its 52-week high and has not received too much love for the last five years. It has gone absolutely nowhere, and in fact, the trend has actually been heading lower. This is a name I initially added on August 1, 2025, so it is still a relatively fresh name in the portfolio. At that time, I added shares at $74.80—and now I get to add at a bit lower price.

Wall Street has an average price target of $81, and Morningstar puts fair value at around $79 (it isn't covered directly from one of their analysts, but the price is based on an algorithm). It is important to note that when I say AWR looks like a relative value, it is relative to itself historically. One of the areas to be otherwise critical of this name is that it is a 'boring' water utility but still trades at a forward P/E of 21.13x. That's nearly what the S&P 500 Index has been stretched to for something that isn't expected to grow as fast.

So I do believe that is a valid criticism, but I would highlight that it has traded at an average of 30x throughout the last decade.

With short-term rates coming down and long-term rates stabilizing, I believe that makes AWR look more appealing. This is because it makes the nearly 3% dividend yield look more appealing in a lower rate environment. While money markets and CDs still pay above this amount for now, it may not be by the end of the year. Those also don't see their payouts increase during the duration of holding them either; with AWR, you are looking at a dividend king with 71 years of consecutive dividend increases.

The last bump was a solid 8.3% raise, with the last decade's average CAGR coming in at the same level.

#3 Paychex (PAYX)

PAYX is another name that has been beaten down lately and is down nearly 30% from its high—sending back all of what looked like it was going to be a solid surge in price after staying flat for a couple of years prior to that.

In terms of the dividend, this has pushed the dividend yield to ~3.8%. The last 10-year CAGR has come in around 10%. They have 12 years of consecutive increases, and actually a bit longer, going back to the Global Financial Crisis. At that time, they froze the dividend for several years. In 2012, they paid an additional 2 dividends at the end of the year as an "accelerated" dividend ahead of a tax hike for high-income earners.

With this said, I believe there are two factors at play here for pressure on the share price.

The first, as a payroll and HR operator for mid-sized and small businesses, they are incredibly sensitive to the economy. In 2025, despite what seemed like an overall solid year of growth and consumers continuing to remain resilient, there was actually a significant increase in the number of bankruptcies. This includes businesses and non-businesses, with levels that we haven't seen since 2010. If that trend continues, it can be bad news for a company like PAYX.

The second factor, in my opinion, is that Paychex bought Paycor last year, and that deal closed near the beginning of the year. The deal itself is generally likely to be positive as it helps to provide more sales and is expected to be accretive to adjusted EPS in this fiscal year; at least that is what they noted. Analysts are generally expecting that as well. We see a nice lift in both sales and EPS above what the company had been seeing.

It is also worth noting that shares trade at nearly 21x forward earnings, but this has generally been a faster grower, which is why it historically traded closer to 27x.

However, what this also did was substantially increase debt, and that muddies up their balance sheet. This was one of the companies that was a lean, mean, clean balance sheet machine. Until 2019, the company had no debt at all, which is when they bought Oasis Outsourcing and took on some financing to get the deal done.

With that small amount of debt, they barely went net debt/EBITDA positive, and they did, but only very briefly. With this move, it is no longer the very clean balance sheet we saw previously.

With all that said, we can clearly see that it still isn't a substantial amount of indebtedness. 1.32x net debt to EBITDA is very tame; generally, 3x is often considered excessive for most traditional corporations. Still, I think it is the fact that it isn't as clean a balance sheet as it was, and we are heading into what could be an economic slowdown. At least, if the labor market continues to weaken and we continue to see a rise in bankruptcies.

I believe that taking advantage of this latest decline in the name can still be a solid play over the longer term, even if the next year the stock trades flat or falls even further. Of course, it may or may not, and shares could head higher from here, and that unknown is why I'm comfortable adding some to this position anyway.

data.title: Barclays: Is There Still Value Ahead Following A Strong Share Price Rally?
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, I turned bullish on Barclays PLC (BCS) back in May last year as the bank was reporting a very good operating performance, and its valuation was much lower compared to other European banks, which didn’t seem to be warranted. This BCS recommendation has been quite positive, given that its shares have increased by more than 51% since then and have clearly outperformed the SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) during the same period, as can be seen in the next graph.

Since my last article on Barclays, the bank has reported its earnings related to the third quarter and nine months of 2025, which have maintained a positive operating momentum and resulted in slightly improved guidance for the full year 2025.

Indeed, Barclays reported total revenues of £7.2 billion in Q3 2025, up by 9% YoY, and its profitability measured by the return on tangible equity (RoTE) ratio was 10.6% in the quarter. During the first nine months of the year (9M 2025), its RoTE ratio was 12.3%, which is significantly higher than its target for the full year set at the beginning of the year of around 11%.

Due to its stronger performance than expected over the past few quarters, Barclays increased its guidance and now expects to achieve a RoTE ratio above 11% for the full year 2025, while for 2026 it is expecting a RoTE ratio above 12%. This shows that Barclays seems to be positive about its business outlook in the short term and expects earnings to keep growing over the next few quarters, as a higher RoTE ratio is mainly justified by higher profitability and not a lower level of capital, as the bank maintained unchanged its CET1 ratio target between 13-14% over the medium term.

Therefore, higher profitability is supported both by higher revenues expected ahead and good cost control, as the bank has a cost-cutting program ongoing that is already generating significant expense reductions, and this trend should continue over the next year.

Regarding revenues, there has been some slowdown in recent months, which led to stable revenues on a quarterly basis during Q3 2025, largely explained by lower revenues in capital markets activities following an extraordinarily strong Q2 due to higher customer activity related to the U.S. trade war back in April. On the other hand, revenues in the Retail & Corporate segment performed better, up by 6% quarter-on-quarter, which is encouraging for revenue growth ahead, as this unit has a more recurring revenue profile over the medium to long term.

Another supportive factor for increasing revenues in Q4 2025 and also during 2026 is the bank’s structural hedge income, which helps to offset pressure from lower interest rates in the U.K. While the notional amount of the structural hedge book has been stable at about £232-233 billion over the last year, the average yield of this book was only about 2.5% over the last quarter. This rate is still lower than what the bank can get when reinvesting maturing securities, explaining why the structural hedge income is expected to maintain an upward trend over the next few quarters despite lower market rates.

Indeed, the average hedge duration of this book increased as Barclays reinvested maturities toward longer-dated securities, pushing the average duration of the structural hedge book to around 3.5 years at the end of last quarter (from 3 years at the end of last June). The bank has reinvested at yields between 3.5-4% in recent months, leading to higher income from the structural hedge book, a trend that is expected to persist during 2026 and eventually 2027. This is expected to generate some £5.9 billion net interest income (NII) in 2025, representing an increase of £1.2 billion from the previous year, and should generate around £7 billion in 2026, which represents about 37% of its total NII.

This shows that the structural hedge income is quite important for Barclays to maintain top-line growth ahead, especially during a period of declining interest rates by the Bank of England. The last cut was made in December to 3.75%, reaching the lowest level since early 2023, and more cuts are expected over the coming months as inflation has eased in the U.K., and the central bank thinks the monetary policy no longer needs to be as restrictive.

Despite headwinds from lower rates, the market is expecting Barclays to report total revenues of nearly £29 billion in 2025 (+8% YoY) and around £30.6 billion in 2026 (+5.4% YoY), showing that Barclays’ interest rate sensitivity has been reduced in large part due to its efforts to increase the structural hedge income. Another potential positive factor for the bank’s NII resilience during 2026 is that the average cost of deposits usually reacts more rapidly to lower market rates than asset yields, being an important factor for the bank to protect its business margins.

Beyond higher revenues expected over the coming quarters, another positive factor for higher profitability is the bank’s efforts to cut costs and improve efficiency across the group. During the first nine months of 2025, Barclays was able to reach more than £500 million in cost savings, which was its target for the full year, showing that it has done a good job in this respect. This is quite important to offset other cost pressures from inflation and regulation, but it was not enough to improve efficiency compared to previous quarters.

Indeed, its cost-to-income ratio was 63% in the last quarter, compared to 61% in the same quarter of 2024, being penalized by the integration of Tesco Bank and extraordinary costs of £235 million related to the motor finance issue in the U.K.

This issue is related to hidden or undisclosed commissions in car finance agreements, especially through dealerships, and can become the largest potential regulatory issue in the U.K. following the PPI scandal some years ago. The motor finance issue may cost between £8-13 billion for U.K. banks and specialized financial services companies in the country in redress costs, of which Lloyds Banking Group (LYG) and Close Brothers (CBGPY) should be the most exposed to this issue. Indeed, Lloyds has already made provisions around £1 billion related to this issue, while Barclays’ provisions amounted to £325 million at the end of last quarter, showing that it is much less exposed to potential compensation costs. Nevertheless, the regulator has not yet released final details on how much customers will be compensated, something that should happen in the coming months, and payments should start to be made by mid-year. While I expect this issue to have a manageable impact on Barclays’ financial figures in the short term, it creates some uncertainty about its total costs over the coming quarters and could have some negative impact on the bank’s profitability ahead that should not be neglected by investors.

Regarding credit quality, there were no significant surprises in the third quarter of 2025, even though its loan loss ratio increased a little bit to 57 basis points (bps) of average loans, still within its guidance of 50-60 bps loan loss ratio over the cycle. In U.S. credit cards, credit quality also remained quite stable, which is a good sign that U.S. consumers continue to show strength. However, recent suggestions of a credit card cap rate of 10% in the U.S. may be a negative factor for Barclays’ profitability in this segment, even if credit quality remains solid during 2026.

This unit accounts for about 11% of Barclays’ overall profits, and an interest rate cap would certainly pressure the returns of the U.S. Consumer Bank unit, which reported a RoTE ratio of only 9.4% in 9M 2025, and could potentially put at risk the bank’s target of a RoTE ratio above 12% in 2026. While it’s not certain a cap will become law, this is another risk that investors should be aware of.

The combination of higher revenues but also higher costs and relatively stable credit costs has resulted in a profit of nearly £5 billion during the first nine months of 2025, up by 14% YoY, while for the full year the street expects the bottom line to reach close to £6.2 billion (+16% YoY). For 2026, current expectations are clearly bullish given that analysts are expecting Barclays to earn around £7.3 billion in the year, which represents annual growth of 18%. I see this as somewhat optimistic, and potentially earnings can be revised downwards over the coming months, as I see high-single-digit growth as much more likely considering current headwinds and risk related to rates in the U.K., potential regulatory costs, and caps on credit card rates in the U.S.

On the capital front, Barclays remains well capitalized with a CET1 ratio above 14% at the end of last September, being comfortably above its own internal target. This means the bank has an excess capital position and can continue its strategy of returning capital to shareholders both through dividends and share buybacks, while also pursuing business growth through targeted acquisitions.

Regarding its valuation, Barclays is currently trading at about 1.08x book value, a much higher valuation compared to when I last analyzed it (0.84x book value at the time) due to a strong share price performance lately. As can be seen in the next graph, Barclays’ shares have re-rated strongly over the past year but are still trading at a discount to some of its U.K. peers, such as Lloyds or HSBC Holdings Plc (HSBC), that trade at 1.3x and 1.7x book value, respectively.

Conclusion

While Barclays PLC has performed quite well over the past year and is trading nowadays at a much higher valuation multiple than compared to a couple of years ago, I still think there is some upside potential ahead due to a discounted valuation compared to peers and also considering the improved profitability level that is expected ahead.

Having said that, there are certainly some risks in the short term that shouldn’t be neglected by investors that could derail the bank’s investment case, being quite important to analyze the bank’s guidance and outlook for the remainder of this year when it reports its annual earnings related to 2025 at the beginning of next month to see if its investment case remains strong or if investors should take profits. Thus, I maintain my Buy rating for the time being but may review to Hold in the future as I see the risk-reward proposition less attractive than it was some months ago.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Endava: Margins Are The Stock's Downfall, But They Should Recover
data.description:
I last wrote about Endava Plc (NYSE:DAVA) in March 2025, framing it as a recovery setup after what I believed was a cyclical reset year. The stock was down 45% in the preceding year when I published that article, and it is down another ¬70% in the 11+ months since then. So, I’m taking a step back to review the business’s performance in that period and reassess it as it exists today.

To be clear, the company hasn’t blown itself up by losing its customer base, abandoning its strategy, or stumbling into a balance sheet crisis. What happened was almost unavoidable: margins dropped. The question I’m answering with this thesis is whether Endava is being priced as a business that is permanently impaired or one that is going through an extended but survivable period of adjustment.

What Actually Changed Since March?

Endava financials: then vs now

£ millions (except margins)

FY24A

FY25A

FY26E (guide)

Revenue

~740

772

735–752

YoY growth

(7%)

+4%

(3%) to flat

Gross margin

~25.5%

~25.6%

~22% (implied)

The thing that stands out to me when looking at Endava, besides the numbers trajectory over the last few years, is that the recovery we expected in 2025 didn’t show up. The company basically missed all the guides for the latter half of FY25, which is why its revenue ended up falling short by around £23 million for the full year.

Yes, the revenue was relatively stable across the year, but growth was low and uneven across quarters. Even more importantly, it depended a lot on verticals that were under pressure themselves. In this case, it was dealing with smaller deal sizes, and the tailwind I was most looking forward to seeing, digital transformation spending, just didn’t come back as I expected.

That’s one thing, but it was almost a tale of two halves in the year. In H1, it recorded more revenue and very poor margins. Then it’s almost like something switched in H2, because revenue was lower, but the margins were far better. In my original thesis, I just assumed that revenue would keep rising and margins would follow, but that didn’t happen. And at those margin levels, it is hard to justify being bullish on a company that was operating well below a quality nearshore services model.

I believe that the margins, more than anything else, have been the downfall of Endava’s stock price. The company was doing the right things with its repositioning and presence in key markets like North America, but when your wins don’t flow into the bottom line, you can’t ask people to just believe in the narrative.

AI is a good example of this. Endava’s gameplan is to move from being a “mid-tier services firm to an AI-native enterprise enabler,” as I put it in the March article, and the company has made real progress on this front. It now has an “AI native change delivery lifecycle” branded DavaFlow, more deals with customers on the AI front, and even an OpenAI partnership to boot, per management’s words during the Q1 FY26 earnings call. The problem is that most of the work it is managing to pull off with AI is showing up as advisory, experimentation, or early-stage deployments. Those developments make sense from a strategic standpoint, but they haven’t been the growth driver I hoped for.

They aren’t offsetting the company’s weakness in more traditional discretionary work or providing the kind of repeatable, scalable delivery revenue that will change its earnings profile. And we haven’t yet seen even one particularly major deal that changes how we should value the business moving forward. All of these things put together have really dragged the narrative, and the stock, too, which is why we’re here now.

Unfortunately, it doesn’t look like FY26 will be any better. Q1 results missed expectations again, and management is guiding for a slowdown from FY25’s already uninspiring performance. Given this context, I think the next logical step is deciding whether Endava’s strategy can still translate into acceptable growth and margins over a reasonable timeframe, or not.

Valuation

Endava’s share price today is $6.6, and its equity value is ~$350 million, while its enterprise value is ~$611 million. The company also ended FY25 with ~£181 million in non-current borrowings and ~£59 million in cash, so its real net debt was around £122 million at the time. If I use a GBP/USD spot of ¬1.34, which is today’s rate, the net debt is roughly $160 million.

FY25 revenue was £772.3 million, and with the same GBP/USD spot, that comes out to just over $1 billion. Compared to today’s EV of $611 million, it means that Endava’s EV/Sales ratio is 0.6x. In my opinion, that multiple is the market saying that it doesn’t see the company’s fundamentals and trajectory changing anytime soon.

However, I think that assumption is too harsh. The one thing that will materially move this stock is its margins, and I know those aren’t great now. But I believe that we don’t need to see historical peak-level margins to justify a re-rating in the near-term. The company just needs to show us that it can get back to a mid-cycle, “not embarrassing” margin structure. That could be 29% gross and 9% operating, for a start, and it can achieve those if it just manages to execute decently.

Now, I’ll use those metrics to paint a picture of what I’d call a reasonable performance. Management is guiding for FY26 revenue of £735M–£752M. Let’s take the midpoint as £743.5 million, and using the same spot as earlier, we get ~$1 billion in revenue. If Endava can get back to a 9% operating margin over the next year or so, that is $90 million of EBIT on a $1 billion revenue base.

I don’t think that the market will pay an EBIT multiple of 18x for Endava, at least not in its current state, so I’ll go with a more conservative multiple of 12x. That gives us an implied EV of ~$1.08 billion, and when I take out the net debt from earlier ($160 million), we end up with a market cap of $920 million. Given that it has around 53 million shares outstanding, fair value comes out to just over $17/share.

What I just outlined isn’t an unrealistic scenario. Sure, I know that the company may still miss its revenue target for FY26, but I’m keeping faith, seeing as it has already delivered a quarter of the figure in Q1. I’d also like to point out that Endava doesn’t need to do much to prove that it is still a credible services operator. It is just going through a particularly rough patch.

I can still cut expectations, to be clear, so we can see what a baseline price looks like. If EBIT only actually goes back up to 6.5% and the market gives it a 10x EV/EBIT, the company would record $60 million and have an EV value of $650 million. The same debt value of $160 million is there, so its equity value will be $490 million. At these levels, the stock price will still sit at ~$9.2. That is still a 30% upside from today’s price, and it is based on my assuming that the company doesn’t ever earn back the higher quality multiples from before. That is unlikely, but I laid out those calculations to highlight how mispriced the stock is.

What I’m Watching Moving Forward: Risks

For me, the Buy case in Endava rests on four things, and they all tie directly back to the margins.

The first one is utilization. The fact is that Endava’s margin model only works when its utilization moves meaningfully higher. So, I’m looking for consistency, especially on a quarterly basis. I’d like to see utilization improving sequentially over multiple quarters because that tells me that it has more demand. More demand means more money, so it can also start to offset the fixed costs. Failing that, it is very difficult for me to stay bullish on its margin recovery.

I also want to see better economics, generally speaking. What I mean by this is that pricing discipline and deal quality must improve. The company has been prioritizing volume over pricing economics over the last year, and that has really affected the numbers. I understand the rationale, but it is not a sustainable way to grow the business. I want to hear from management and see it in the numbers that it is signing new work at better margins, even if it means we will see slower growth. We have seen slower growth over the last few years, after all.

Then we have the mix, both in terms of the verticals and geography. For the latter, North America is still the biggest revenue contributor, but it needs to do more on the margin side of things. With verticals, it looks like healthcare is definitely growing quickly, but I want to see Endava actually deliver higher-value work there, as well as in other regulated industries. Again, it comes down to pricing.

The last thing I will touch on here is cost discipline. The company has spent a boatload of cash on expanding its delivery capabilities, especially in AI, along with headcount, and we haven’t seen the demand that justifies those expenses. So, spending more money without seeing better results has been the name of the game for a while now, and that needs to change. If it stays the same, then the margins will continue to suffer, and we’ll be back to having the conversation around whether Endava’s business model is working.

The best part of Endava’s case is that we can observe these things every quarter and decide what it’s worth from there. For now, I’d say it’s very obvious why the stock is cheap, and that’s it.

It’s A Measured Buy, For Now

Endava today is not in the same position as it was when I first wrote about it in March, but it is also not what the market is valuing it as at this point. I’ve discussed the reasons for the stock’s drawdown and the upside that exists, using what I consider to be reasonable assumptions, and I believe that the upside is far greater than the downside from here. It’s a matter of patience and discipline, in my opinion, and I’m recommending a Buy on the stock.

data.title: China Automotive Systems: No Breakthrough Is No Reason To Walk Away
data.description:
China Automotive Systems Inc (CAAS), a supplier of power steering components and systems for the automotive industry, has been putting the bulls to the test by trying their patience. The stock has basically gone sideways for well over a year in the charts because it is hamstrung by what appears to be resistance, which has prevented CAAS from moving beyond a certain point, even though there were a number of instances when it appeared CAAS was going to break through and move higher. This inability to move higher beyond a certain point is likely to cause at least some to give up and move on to greener pastures, but there is nonetheless reason to be optimistic that CAAS has a more than decent shot at breaking through on a future attempt, especially since headwinds have recently receded in strength.

CAAS gets rejected again

A previous article from October 2025 written by myself mentioned how CAAS was facing a number of headwinds, which had not only grown in strength in prior months, but also increased in number. For instance, supply chain disruptions caused by internal problems at Nexperia, a leading supplier of automotive chips, had erupted shortly before, which became an issue for automakers because it led to a shortage of needed inputs, including at automakers sourcing components from CAAS. Yet, while the possibility existed CAAS would have to deal with some sort of adverse impact, CAAS was arguably in a position to handle these headwinds. I thus decided to rate CAAS a buy in the article.

The chart above shows how CAAS has tested the patience of the bulls. CAAS is actually off to a good start in 2026, but similar to what happened a number of times before, the stock had its way higher cut off because the rally only managed to get as high as $4.98 on January 12, before CAAS pulled back. This is reminiscent of what happened a number of times before, including in September 2025, March 2025 and as far back as October 2024.

In all these instances, as shown in the chart above, the stock struggled to go higher in or around the $4.80-5.20 region, brief spikes notwithstanding. The stock last closed at $4.63, which means CAAS has already appreciated by a respectable 8.7% this early into 2026. However, the move and the resulting gains do not mean that much since they are offset by the fact that the same resistance that has held CAAS back since October 2024, or well over a year, remains in place. CAAS can try again, but for now the road higher looks blocked off.

Why headwinds have lost strength recently

While CAAS has not been able to overcome the resistance imposed on it, there are some encouraging developments worth noting that likely contributed to the recent rise in the stock. They could also eventually allow for another and perhaps more successful attempt at breaking through, or at least improve the likelihood of it happening. For starters, some of the headwinds mentioned in the previous article from October have receded to a certain degree.

One headwind mentioned was the issue of automakers having problems sourcing automotive chips from Nexperia, which had the potential to slow or even halt production of automobiles. This would in turn affect all other suppliers, including CAAS. However, while the internal dispute at Nexperia remains unresolved, the automotive industry has found a workaround of sorts by buying wafers directly from Nexperia Europe and have them shipped to Nexperia China for further processing to get finished products. This process is not ideal, but it should in theory allow for production to proceed smoothly, provided all relevant parties stick to it.

Another headwind that has receded in strength is the issue of rare earth elements. While China’s export controls on rare earth remain in place, trade talks between the U.S.-China have resulted in increased shipments of rare earth elements to qualified importers. So it appears the likelihood of supply chain problems due to rare earth has receded, at least at this time.

Why there is reason to be bullish CAAS

It needs mentioning that both the Nexperia issue and the rare earth issue could potentially flare up again. Still, assuming both issues remain subdued, then that could help pave the way for the stock to move higher, especially in combination with other supporting factors acting as catalysts. It just so happens that CAAS has a number of attributes that are likely to convince potential buyers that CAAS deserves to trade at a higher valuation and is thus worth betting on. This could cause them to get in on CAAS, pushing the stock price higher.

For instance, the automotive industry has been shaken up by a number of headwinds in the past year or so. Nexperia and rare earth were mentioned earlier, but other issues like U.S. tariffs on finished products like autos and tariffs on key inputs needed like steel and aluminum have also given the automotive industry a headache.

However, it is encouraging to see how despite all the problems in the automotive industry. CAAS itself has seen minimal impact from it all. On the contrary, CAAS has been able to do rather well considering the less than ideal circumstances. In the most recent or Q3 FY2025 report, for instance, CAAS posted strong results after it grew revenue by 17.7% YoY to $193.2M, which resulted in adjusted EPS of $0.32, up from $0.18 one year ago.

In the first three quarter of FY2025, revenue grew by 16.1% YoY to $536.5M and adjusted EPS grew by 17.4% YoY to $0.81, even with all the various issues the automotive industry had to contend with during this time. In a further sign of how well CAAS is coping with the current environment, CAAS raised its outlook by forecasting FY2025 revenue to end up at $730M by the end of the year, which implies Q4 FY2025 revenue of $193.5M since revenue in Q1-Q3 FY2025 totaled $536.5M.

This would make FY2025 the fifth consecutive year of growth since FY2020, the last year of no growth, which can be attributed to the lockdowns caused by COVID-19. My estimate is that FY2025 EPS will likely end up at around $1.13, assuming Q4 FY2025 EPS stays flat QoQ at $0.32. This implies CAAS is trading at just 4.09x estimated FY2025 earnings with the stock last closing at $4.63.

It also implies EPS managed a CAGR of 17.87% in FY2022-2025, using FY2022 EPS of $0.69 as the base and eFY2025 EPS of $1.13. At the same time, it is prudent to leave room for the possibility CAAS might be affected by the headwinds the automotive industry is facing, which could affect earnings growth in the coming years and result in a somewhat lower growth rate.

If we keep projecting and assume CAAS is able to keep growing revenue and earnings at the demonstrated double-digit pace in the next three years or FY2025-2028, but lower than before at an estimated CAGR of 10% to account for the potential impact from industry headwinds, then this implies CAAS is available at a PEG of less than one, or 0.41x, with CAAS trading at a P/E of 4.09x, which the methodology espoused by Peter Lynch suggests makes CAAS undervalued relative to growth.

The same methodology points to a fair value much higher than the current stock price of $11.30, or $1.13 times 10, for CAAS, assuming a 10% CAGR for earnings, which takes into account what CAAS could conceivably achieve under present industry conditions and what it has historically demonstrated to be capable of.

CAAS also looks undervalued based on the balance sheet. According to the Q3 FY2025 report, CAAS has cash and cash equivalents totaling $139.42M, which is close to its market cap of $139.7M. CAAS is actually valued at less than half its book value of $12.56 and the stock trading at $4.63, which equals CAAS trading at a price-to-book of 0.37x. This also points to a fair value well above the current stock price, or more like in the low teens similar to the previous method. In short, both the income statement and the balance sheet are supportive of the case for long CAAS.

Structural shift in the automotive world favor CAAS as an automotive supplier

There is another reason as to why CAAS is likely to trade at a higher valuation than it does now. It’s true that CAAS is exposed to a number of automakers that are struggling for a variety of reasons, but this is something that CAAS can handle. For instance, Stellantis (STLA) is CAAS’ top customer, having contributed about a fifth of FY2024 revenue, and Stellantis has not only been affected by the headwinds mentioned earlier, like rare earth supplies, but it has also experienced pressure on its competitive position in the global auto market, including in sales.

On the other hand, roughly two-thirds of revenue at CAAS is derived from China, including from such automakers like BYD, a name that has risen in recent years. The significance of this is that automakers from China are gaining global market share, which should drive growth at CAAS. Chinese automakers, for instance, managed to rank number one in terms of vehicle sales in 2025. They surpassed Japanese automakers, which held the number one spot for two decades.

This growth should rub off on suppliers like CAAS. While sales to some customers like STLA might suffer, this should be offset by growth elsewhere, resulting in little to no overall impact. The recent earnings report are encouraging in that sense to those long CAAS because they are consistent with this dynamic taking place. CAAS, which is more exposed to the better-performing and stronger part of the automotive industry and less to the weaker part, has an advantage.

In comparison, not all suppliers in the automotive world are as fortunate as CAAS. For instance, while there are many suppliers, the leading supplier of power steering systems is Bosch. Bosch is much larger than CAAS and it supplies many more parts than just power steering to automakers all around the world, but Bosch is reportedly expected to miss its sales target for 2025.

This after Bosch already took various measures throughout 2025, including mass layoffs, to deal with the various problems facing not just the company, but the industry as a whole. In contrast, while CAAS faces many of the same problems Bosch and others are seeing, it has still managed to grow by double digits. This is a clear sign of strength at CAAS and something to be bullish about.

Is CAAS without risk?

While CAAS has managed to deal with unfavorable industry conditions, the risk is that there is a further deterioration in the automotive world to such an extent that it starts to adversely affect even CAAS. While headwinds have receded to a certain extent recently, they are still very much out there. This possibility is the biggest risk facing CAAS in the short term. Anyone long CAAS needs to be aware of this.

There is also the risk that CAAS as a U.S.-listed stock that primarily operates in China could get tangled up by increased tensions between the U.S. and China. Most of the shares are also held by a small number of insiders, primarily the founder of CAAS, which some may see as a risk because the company is effectively under their control.

Investor takeaways

Most of the leading automakers, whether in the U.S., Europe or Japan, have been affected by industry headwinds. Tariffs, for instance, has and continues to be an issue for automakers, but some like those in China have been less impacted because they do not much of a presence in the U.S. Yet automakers in China are not without their own problems.

The biggest one is arguably what the Chinese government has referred to as “involution”, which basically entails the Chinese auto market in a state of extreme competition. The market is characterized by persistent price cuts by competitors to retain market share, which makes it difficult to make profits.

Many Chinese automakers have been able to offset whatever issues they are facing at home by turning to export markets. This has allowed them to gain market share because they have been able to grow faster than the traditional stalwarts. CAAS, which mainly supplies to these automakers, is reaping the benefits of these structural and therefore long-term changes taking place in the automotive industry.

This dynamic provides a solid base for long CAAS because it provides CAAS with the catalyst that can allow it to keep growing both the top and the bottom line at the double-digit pace recorded in the past 3-5 years. As detailed earlier, CAAS arguably deserves to trade significantly higher than it does now, possibly into the low teens, based on projected earnings growth in the income statement and book value on the balance sheet.

So while there is no denying that CAAS has been essentially stuck for over a year as it has been unable to decisively get past $5.00 or so, there is also reason to remain optimistic that CAAS can get it done by breaking through the resistance standing in the way and moving higher. The fact that CAAS has not just shown resilience with so much going against it in the industry, but even thrive is very encouraging to see and an attribute worth having. I am therefore bullish on CAAS with the above in mind, even though CAAS has admittedly not appreciated by all that much in the past 18 months or so.

Bottom line, CAAS has yet to really deliver in term of returns. Yet there is reason to remain confident it can happen down the line. CAAS has a number of things going for it. While it’s true they have yet to translate into stock gains, it is worth sticking with long CAAS because there is a good chance it will eventually happen.

data.title: Intel: Weak Guidance Matters More Than Reasonable Earnings
data.description:
As Intel Corporation's (INTC) shareholder, I was amazed by the market's latest activity driving the stock price up by a lot. I saw Intel's stock price grow by nearly 50% just over the course of the last month. To be honest, I found it quite surprising - yes I knew the market was expecting a reasonable earnings report, but let's be honest - buying INTC at these prices was risky business.

The last time I covered Intel I declared it a Hold as its stock price hiked after many months of poor performance. I established my long position in May 2025 and as I explained in my last coverage, I trimmed. I did not sell the whole position as I wanted to participate in INTC's further story.

As I was watching its stock price nearing $50 per share just before a very important press release, I sold another stake at just below $50 per share. Don't get me wrong - I still own some shares that account for around 40% of my initial position.

I feel quite lucky with the decision I made as Intel's pre-market price indicates that the Mr. Market is not happy with the earnings release. Intel is likely to open at over 10% downside from yesterday's close, which I consider a sharp reaction. Still, I think it's justified given two factors:

  1. I believe the Q4 performance itself was fine, especially as Intel topped expectations by a bit. The issue lies with its modest guidance for the upcoming quarter.
  2. I don't think previous stock price growth was justified, making this sharp drop quite a natural reaction to disappointing guidance.

I will go through this step by step, starting with INTC's earnings review, but I want to make one thing clear. I still own ~40% of my initial position in Intel, despite trimming two times. I intend to hold onto this stake unless valuation or business change materially. Intel remains a Hold for me.

Intel had a reasonable Q4 2025 performance

I believe many investors have mixed feelings about INTC's results - both revenue and gross margins were down on year-over-year basis. Revenue dropped by 4.1% - I think some may take it as another bad sign, but I don't think that to be the case.

What's important to me is that INTC's quarterly revenue was much higher than that delivered in Q1-Q2 2025, which was also true for Q3 2025. Yes, there's also seasonality at play here, but I would be very concerned if Q4 2025 was closer to the performance of the first half of 2025. As far as gross margins go, of course I'd prefer to see higher rather than lower levels, but I'd like to highlight that 37.9% was sufficient to allow Intel to generate ~$1.2B of operating income.

Even Intel's annual revenue was down when compared to 2024. But the management has a reasoning behind it - let me paste it right below quoting from the Earnings Call:

For the full year, revenue was $52.9 billion, down slightly year-over-year due to constraints across our own manufacturing network and with external suppliers which limited growth, especially in the second half.

I think there's another positive note to these results - they were above the management's guidance shared last year. INTC has beaten its own expectations in terms of both revenue and gross profit:

Fourth quarter revenue was $13.7 billion at the high end of the range we provided in October.

(...)

Q4 marks the fifth consecutive quarter of revenue above our guidance even as we navigate industry-wide supply constraints for our key products. Non-GAAP gross margin came in at 37.9%, approximately 140 basis points ahead of guidance (...)

We delivered fourth quarter non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.15 versus our guidance of $0.08 and driven by higher revenue, stronger gross margins and continued spending discipline.

Here's what I see - revenue higher by $0.4B vs the outlook shared in October, higher gross margin (~140 bps), and nearly double the expected EPS. Given Intel's struggles over the last couple of years, I have to admit - I'm satisfied with Intel's Q4 2025 performance.

Unfortunately, the management shared quite a modest guidance

And here's where I found a lot of bad news. Intel's guidance for Q1 of 2026 is far from optimistic. We discussed three key metrics above in terms of Q4 2025 and I presented them in a relatively positive light, but this ends here.

EPS is expected to get back to $0 - down $0.13 at y/o/y basis and $0.15 at quarterly basis. Gross margin continues to shrink and I see the management expects it to reach 34.5% in Q1 2026. Will that be enough to ensure INTC's operating profitability? I doubt that, especially given a break-even level of expected EPS.

The range for revenue outlook is quite wide from where I'm standing - I see the management expects $11.7B-12.7B, which would be down by $0.5B at midpoint.

That's what I was afraid of and that concern was one of the main reasons I sold another stake of my shares. It's not like Intel can make a turn-around story at such a short time. It could happen, but it will take longer than the market expected (judging by recent dynamic stock price growth).

What's the reason behind these expectations? Here's the management's take on that - I'm pasting it below:

Now turning to guidance. During the second half of 2025, we supported strong demand for our products with intra-quarter wafer production and inventory on hand. As we enter 2026, our buffer inventory is depleted and the mix shift in wafers towards servers, which began in Q3 will not come out of fab until late Q1 '26. As a result, and as we stated last quarter, our internal supply constraints are most acute in Q1.

In light of these dynamics, we are forecasting a Q1 revenue range of $11.7 billion to $12.7 billion. The midpoint of $12.2 billion reflects a lower end of seasonal Q1.

My conclusion is simple - beware of dynamic stock price increases but don't sell in panic

As I said at the beginning - Intel is a Hold for me. I'm very suspicious of rapid stock price increases in turn-around cases such as Intel - I recommend you the same approach.

That's why I did not believe in the $50+ stock price. Not at this point, the business hasn't been restructured enough for such a high valuation to be sustainable. That's why I trimmed the second time.

I believe it could be the case at some point - that's why I'm going to hold onto some shares, but my position is much lower than it used to be originally. Intel has much more work to do, much more improvement to show in its financials -ideally I'd like to see such improvements sustainable and not one-off scenarios in any given quarter.

I agree with Wall Street Analysts who, on average, expect Intel's stock price to decrease by ~18%. I'm pasting the chart below for you to review.

The upside potential is limited for now, but I believe those who are patient will be able to benefit with Intel. At the same time, I don't recommend buying at these levels - investors should give Intel time to turn things around. Frankly, INTC remains a Hold for me.

data.title: MUB: Largest Investment-Grade Muni Bond ETF, But Not A Buy At These Levels
data.description:
It's been a bit less than a year since I've covered the iShares National Muni Bond ETF (MUB), the largest muni bond ETF in the market, and something of a stand-in for quality munis as an asset class. Last time I covered the fund I argued that t-bills and investment-grade corporate bonds offered higher after-tax income for the vast majority of investors, making MUB and most of the quality muni ETFs sub-par investments. Since then, rates for t-bills and most bonds are down, muni bond rates flat. Spreads have narrowed between munis and other types of bonds, although perhaps not by enough to justify an investment in MUB. I maintain my hold rating, but the situation could easily change in the next few months.

MUB and Munis - After-Tax Yields

MUB is an investment-grade muni bond index ETF. Due to its broad index and diversified portfolio, it is something of an industry benchmark and should perform in-line with quality munis as an asset class.

For these securities, the most important consideration is their after-tax yields. Insofar as these are good and higher-than-average, the investments merit a buy. If not, then a sell or a hold. As after-tax yields are strongly dependent on the specific circumstances of these investors one can't draw absolute conclusions for these securities, but some general trends do seem clear.

Right now, MUB yields 3.2%, slightly lower than most bonds and bond sub-asset classes. Standardized SEC yields are a bit higher, at 3.4%, but the same is true for most types of bonds. Figures for MUB and index ETFs tracking the major bond sub-asset classes are as follows:

Although MUB's dividend yield is somewhat below-average, after-tax yields seem a bit more competitive. As per my calculations, and using SEC yields, the breakeven tax rate for muni bonds versus bonds is 19.0%. Investors facing higher marginal tax rates than that would receive more in income from MUB than BND, and viceversa. Breakevens for muni bonds versus investment-grade corporate bonds are at 30.6%, appreciably higher. I'm not sure which of these rates is more material, or which comparison is fairest, but I lean towards the latter. Bonds in general include treasuries, tilting the scales in favor of MUB due to the quality / low-yield of these securities. Investment-grade corporate bonds are high-quality securities, but not treasuries, which seems a fairer comparison to MUB.

Another way of looking at after-tax yields is through taxable equivalent yields. These basically tell you how much in taxable income do you need to match the income of a tax-free investment, depending on the yield of said investment, and on the marginal tax rate. Table of these is as follows, be sure to focus on the 3.5% tax-free column, closest figure to MUB's 3.4% SEC yield.

In my opinion, the conclusion of the above is quite simple: muni bonds offer higher after-tax yields to investors in the higher tax brackets, not so much for those in the lower brackets. This is generally true, but not always, and there is always value in the confirmation, and in the specific figures here.

On the other hand, muni bonds don't seem to offer materially higher after-tax income than some of my favorite quality ETFs. The best example by far is the Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF (BOXX), a synthetic t-bills options ETF. Returns have been extremely similar to those of t-bills since inception, as should be the case moving forward:

BOXX's strategy aims for zero distributions, although there was a special distribution last year. The lack of distributions means no taxable events, which should allow investors to defer any taxes until they sell their position, presumably at an opportune time. Gains should be taxed at capital gain rates, which are generally lower than income tax rates. BOXX's strategy is more dependent on management execution and, let's say regulatory forbearance, than most. It could stop working in the future, but it has worked quite well in the past, minus that one distribution.

BOXX's returns should, and have, closely track t-bills, which currently yield 3.62%:

That is marginally higher than MUB's 3.4% SEC yield, with BOXX offering a much more stable share price. Comparing their returns since inception shows this quite clearly:

Spreads should flip after another cut, which should occur in the next few months. Even then, MUB would only yield a marginal amount more than BOXX.

As should be clear from the above, MUB offers comparable after-tax income to BOXX, at much greater risk and volatility. Right now, BOXX seems like the broadly superior investment, although could things as rates continue to go down.

Besides BOXX, there are lots of quality income ETFs with yields in the 5.0% - 6.0% range, most of which should offer higher after-tax yields than MUB. As an example, the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY) focuses on quality MBS and ABS, it yields 5.6%, excluding a large distribution in late 2024, with a 5.5% SEC yield. That should mean higher after-tax income for most investors, marginally less for those in the highest tax bracket. CARY's share price and returns are much more stable too, as are total returns since inception.

As there are lots of differences between MUB, CARY, and BOXX, and because after-tax yields are somewhat dependent on the specific circumstances of each investor, perhaps MUB is the better choice for some investors. In my opinion, BOXX and CARY seem broadly superior, and should be of greater interest to more investors.

MUB - Looking Back

I wrote a broadly similar article to this one on MUB last December. Since then, MUB has significantly underperformed most bonds and bond sub-asset classes on a before-tax basis. Underperformance is significant enough that after-tax returns are almost certain to be lower too.

MUB's underperformance was caused by comparatively weak capital gains, with most bonds seeing single-digit gains, MUB close to zero.

The flipside of the above is that before-tax spreads have narrowed, increasing the relative attractiveness of MUB as an investment. The impact here is too small to warrant a change in rating, however.

Besides the above, t-bill rates have declined by 0.75%, decreasing the attractiveness of these investments, including BOXX. Said ETF still seems stronger than MUB overall, but by much less than before. Although this doesn't warrant a change in rating either, the impact is much more significant, and rates could continue to fall.

Conclusion

MUB is the largest muni bond ETF in the market. Although there is nothing significantly wrong with the fund, several of its peers offer competitive after-tax yields. As such, I would not be investing in the fund at the present time.
